synthetic:1325f17516c8abe6eb3226ac6bdd0c.reason: The regex strips complete HTML tags but could leave partial fragments like <script without >. However, description155 is only used as a value in cheerio .attr() calls for meta tag content attributes (lines 169, 188). When cheerio serializes the DOM back to HTML via $.root().html() (line 211), attribute values are properly HTML-escaped — < becomes <, making any surviving fragment inert inside the attribute context. The regex is a content-extraction step, not the security boundary.

synthetic:23bc8e664be1083752cbfe9db34571.reason: The healthcheck script runs as a Docker HEALTHCHECK command inside the container, connecting only to localhost (127.0.0.1) by default. No attacker-controlled input reaches the TLS configuration: the target host is hardcoded to 127.0.0.1 unless overridden by deployment-time environment variables (UPTIME_KUMA_HOST or HOST), which are not attacker-modifiable. The script only performs a simple GET request with no sensitive data transmitted. TLS verification is intentionally disabled to accommodate self-signed certificates commonly used in internal/local deployments.

synthetic:97f49a2ebe9d11bb0379cfa1404f8e.reason: The POST /setup-database route exists only on a temporary Express server that runs exclusively during first-time application setup (when no database is configured). The temporary server shuts down permanently after a single successful setup (lines 285-292). The runningSetup flag (lines 173-176) enforces at-most-one concurrent execution, providing inherent serialization. This is not a persistent production endpoint: it is unreachable on the main server, and the route's containing server is only created and started when setupDatabase.isNeedSetup() returns true (server/server.js:236-238), then destroyed after one success. Rate-limiting a one-shot, self-destructing setup endpoint is unnecessary.

synthetic:d380df956d20339294265b20974daa.reason: This is an NTLM protocol implementation library. The MD5-based HMAC at line 60-61 is mandated by the NTLMv2 protocol specification (Microsoft MS-NLMP). The library has no alternative — replacing MD5 would break protocol interoperability. The username is not a cryptographic secret; it is HMAC message data, while the actual key is the ntlmhash derived from the password. The weak cryptography is a property of the NTLM protocol itself, not a code defect in this implementation.

synthetic:e436763b76d8cff5c9861d5c60f1f3.reason: shake256 is not used as a password hashing function. At the point shake256(user.password) is called in User.createJWT (user.js:45), user.password already contains a bcrypt hash — users are loaded from the database via R.findOne (auth.js:20) where the stored value is a bcrypt hash generated by passwordHash.generate (password-hash.js:10-11 using bcrypt.hash with 10 salt rounds). shake256 is applied to the already-bcrypt-hashed value solely to produce a short fingerprint (16 bytes) embedded in JWTs as the 'h' claim, used to detect password changes and invalidate old tokens (server.js:415). The actual password verification uses bcrypt via passwordHash.verify (auth.js:22, password-hash.js:20-26). SHAKE256 never sees a plaintext password — the scanner is misled by the property name 'password'.

synthetic:eaf07af5225fbbe6de3de162faeac7.reason: The flagged `createNTLMHash` function uses MD4 to compute the NT hash as required by the NTLM authentication protocol specification. This is not a password storage mechanism — it is a transient intermediate step in the NTLM challenge-response handshake. The hash is never persisted; it is used only to compute the NTLM response sent in an HTTP Authorization header. The application's actual password storage uses bcrypt with 10 salt rounds in server/password-hash.js. The MD4 usage here is protocol-mandated, not a password storage choice.

synthetic:f473ce05016d06d2552cde83e29b7c.reason: The rejectUnauthorized: false at line 51 is gated behind notification.teltonikaUnsafeTls (line 49), a user-configurable boolean set via an explicit checkbox in the Teltonika notification settings UI. The UI prominently displays a security warning (teltonikaUnsafeTlsDescription) explaining the man-in-the-middle risk. The code comment at lines 46-48 explains this is intentional for self-signed certificates on local Teltonika routers. The notification configuration is stored in the database and only modifiable by authenticated administrators; an external attacker cannot force this setting. This is an intentional, documented opt-in feature, not an exploitable vulnerability.
